title: Floyd Warshall all pairs
description: All pairs shortest path using Floyd Warshall

examples.explanation: Initialize distance matrix with direct edge weights and INF. Apply Floyd-Warshall: for each intermediate k, update dist[i][j] = min(dist[i][j], dist[i][k] + dist[k][j]). For k=1, path 0→1→2 improves 10 to 5.
Using Floyd-Warshall DP, intermediate vertices progressively reduce path costs. For k=1 and k=2, path 0→1→2→3 reduces distance from 5 to 3.

approaches.name: Brute Force DFS O(V^V)
approaches.summary: For each pair of vertices, explore all possible paths using DFS to find the shortest distance. This is impractical for all but the smallest graphs due to exponential time complexity.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Brute Force DFS All-Pairs Shortest Path
ladders.desc: Find shortest distances between all vertex pairs by exploring every possible path via DFS for each pair.
ladders.explanation: This approach solves the all-pairs shortest path problem by brute-forcing through all possible paths between every pair of vertices. For each source-destination pair (u, v), we perform a DFS from u to v, exploring every possible path in the graph. We maintain the minimum distance found during these explorations. The intuition is simple: if we examine every possible route from u to v, we're guaranteed to find the shortest one. However, this comes at an enormous computational cost—for a complete graph, there are V! possible paths between two vertices, leading to O(V^V) time complexity. The algorithm works by initializing a distance matrix with infinity values. For each pair (u, v), we call DFS(u, v, 0) where the third parameter is the current path distance. The DFS explores all neighbors, accumulating edge weights, and updates the minimum distance when v is reached. We must track visited vertices to avoid cycles (though cycles could be allowed with proper handling). The space complexity is O(V) for the recursion stack and visited tracking. This connects to the key subroutine (L1) which is the DFS exploration between a single pair. Common mistakes include not handling unreachable vertices (keeping infinity), incorrect cycle handling (infinite recursion), and not resetting visited states between DFS calls. The brute-force nature makes this impractical for graphs with more than a handful of vertices, but it's theoretically correct for the problem.

examples.trace: Initialize dist matrix with INF on non-diagonals. Pair (0,1): DFS from 0 to 1. Path 0→1 has distance 2. Update dist[0][1]=2. Pair (0,2): DFS explores 0→2 (dist 10) and 0→1→2 (dist 2+3=5). Minimum is 5. Update dist[0][2]=5. Pair (1,2): DFS from 1 to 2 finds path 1→2 with dist 3. Update dist[1][2]=3. Diagonals remain 0. Final matrix as output.

Initialize matrix. Pair (0,3): DFS explores paths: 0→3 (dist 5), 0→1→2→3 (dist 1+1+1=3). Minimum is 3. Update dist[0][3]=3. Similarly, (0,2): paths 0→1→2 (dist 2) vs direct none, so 2. (0,1): direct 1. (1,3): 1→2→3 gives 2. (1,2): direct 1. (2,3): direct 1. Fill matrix accordingly.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — DFS for Single Pair Shortest Path
ladders.desc: Find shortest path from source to destination using DFS that explores all possible paths.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the core of the brute-force approach: given a source vertex u and destination vertex v, it performs a depth-first search to explore every possible path from u to v, keeping track of the minimum distance found. The intuition is that by exhaustively traversing the graph starting from u, we will eventually reach v through every possible route, allowing us to compare their total distances. The algorithm uses recursion: from the current vertex, it visits all unvisited neighbors (to avoid infinite loops in cyclic graphs), adds the edge weight to the current distance, and recurses. When v is reached, the current distance is compared with the global minimum. Step-by-step: 1. Initialize min_dist = infinity. 2. Define DFS(current, dist, visited): if current == v, update min_dist = min(min_dist, dist). Else, for each neighbor w of current not in visited, call DFS(w, dist + weight(current,w), visited ∪ {current}). 3. Start with DFS(u, 0, empty set). Time complexity is O(V!) in worst case (all permutations of vertices), space is O(V) for recursion stack and visited set. This feeds into L0 by being called for each of the V² pairs. Common mistakes: not using visited set (causing infinite recursion), not handling unreachable cases (min_dist remains infinity), and inefficient pruning. Real code pattern uses adjacency list and recursion with backtracking.

examples.input: Graph: vertices 0,1,2; edges (0,1,2), (1,2,3), (0,2,10). Find shortest from 0 to 2.
examples.trace: Start DFS(0,0,{}). Neighbors: 1 and 2. Explore 0→1: DFS(1,2,{0}). Neighbors of 1: 2. DFS(2,5,{0,1}) reaches destination, update min_dist=5. Backtrack, explore 0→2: DFS(2,10,{0}) reaches destination, update min_dist=min(5,10)=5. End, return 5.

examples.input: Graph: vertices 0,1,2,3; edges (0,1,1), (1,2,1), (2,3,1), (0,3,5). Find shortest from 0 to 3.
examples.trace: DFS(0,0,{}). Paths: 0→3 gives dist 5, update min_dist=5. 0→1→2→3: DFS(0,0,{}) → DFS(1,1,{0}) → DFS(2,2,{0,1}) → DFS(3,3,{0,1,2}) reaches destination, update min_dist=min(5,3)=3. Return 3.

testCases.input: Graph: 3 vertices, no edges. Source 0, dest 2.
Graph: 3 vertices, edges (0,1,1), (1,0,2) directed. Source 0, dest 0.
Graph: 3 vertices, edges (0,1,1), (1,2,1), (2,0,1) cycle. Source 0, dest 2.
Graph: 1 vertex, no edges. Source 0, dest 0.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Exhaustive Path Exploration via Recursion
ladders.desc: Use recursion to traverse all possible paths in a graph from a start vertex.
ladders.explanation: This core logic involves recursively visiting vertices to explore every potential path in a graph. The intuition is that recursion naturally implements depth-first exploration: from a vertex, we dive deep into one neighbor before backtracking to explore others, ensuring we cover all routes. This is key to brute-forcing shortest paths because it enumerates all sequences of vertices. The algorithm: define a recursive function explore(vertex, path, visited) that adds the vertex to the path and visited set, then for each neighbor not in visited, recursively calls explore(neighbor, path + [neighbor], visited ∪ {neighbor}). This generates all simple paths (no repeated vertices) from the start. Time complexity is O(V!) as it generates permutations, space is O(V) for recursion depth and visited storage. This feeds into L1 by providing the mechanism to reach the destination and compute distances. Common mistakes: forgetting to backtrack (modifying visited in place without copy), not handling cycles correctly (visited prevents cycles), and stack overflow for large V. Real code pattern uses list for path and set for visited, with recursive calls.

examples.input: Graph: vertices 0,1,2; edges (0,1), (0,2), (1,2). Start at 0, explore all paths.
examples.trace: Start explore(0, [0], {0}). Neighbors: 1 and 2. First neighbor 1: explore(1, [0,1], {0,1}). Neighbors of 1: 2 (0 visited). explore(2, [0,1,2], {0,1,2}) no unvisited neighbors, path recorded. Backtrack to 0, neighbor 2: explore(2, [0,2], {0,2}) no unvisited neighbors, path recorded. End.

examples.input: Graph: vertices 0,1; edge (0,1). Start at 0, explore all paths.
examples.trace: explore(0, [0], {0}). Neighbor 1: explore(1, [0,1], {0,1}) no neighbors, path recorded. End.

ladders.title: Building Block — Graph Traversal with Visited Tracking
ladders.desc: Traverse a graph while avoiding revisiting vertices using a visited set.
ladders.explanation: This building block involves moving through a graph's vertices while keeping track of which ones have been visited to prevent loops and ensure termination. The intuition is that in graph traversal, especially for pathfinding, we need to avoid infinite cycles; a visited set (or array) marks vertices as seen, so we don't process them again. This is crucial for DFS in brute-force shortest paths because it limits exploration to simple paths. The algorithm: start at a vertex, mark it visited, then recursively or iteratively visit each unvisited neighbor, marking them as visited before proceeding. Step-by-step: 1. Initialize visited set. 2. Define function traverse(vertex): mark vertex visited, for each neighbor not visited, call traverse(neighbor). This ensures each vertex is processed once in a connected component. Time complexity is O(V+E) for traversing all vertices and edges, space is O(V) for visited storage. This feeds into L2 by providing the visited mechanism for exhaustive exploration. Common mistakes: not marking visited before recursion (causing repeats), using visited incorrectly across multiple traversals, and not handling disconnected graphs. Real code pattern uses adjacency list and a set or boolean array.

examples.input: Graph: vertices 0,1,2; edges (0,1), (1,2). Start traversal at 0.
examples.trace: Start at 0, visited={0}. Neighbors: 1 (not visited). Go to 1, visited={0,1}. Neighbors of 1: 2 (not visited). Go to 2, visited={0,1,2}. No unvisited neighbors, backtrack, end.
Start at 0, visited={0}. Neighbors: 1 and 2. Suppose go to 1 first: visit 1, visited={0,1}. Neighbors of 1: none. Backtrack to 0, go to 2: visit 2, visited={0,1,2}. End.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Iterating Over Graph Neighbors
ladders.desc: Access and process all adjacent vertices of a given vertex in a graph.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation involves retrieving and looping through all neighbors of a vertex in a graph, which is fundamental to graph algorithms. The intuition is that graphs represent connections, and to traverse or analyze them, we need to examine which vertices are directly connected to a given vertex. This operation is performed using the graph's representation: typically an adjacency list (list of lists) or adjacency matrix. For a vertex u, we access its neighbor list (e.g., graph[u]) and iterate over each neighbor v. In weighted graphs, we also access the edge weight (e.g., graph[u][v] or as a tuple (v, weight)). Step-by-step: 1. Given graph representation and vertex u. 2. Get neighbors = graph[u] (a list of vertices or (vertex, weight) pairs). 3. For each neighbor in neighbors: process it (e.g., print, add to queue, etc.). Time complexity is O(degree(u)) per vertex, space is O(1) for iteration. This feeds into L3 by enabling traversal to neighbors. Common mistakes: assuming undirected when directed (neighbors may not be reciprocal), incorrect indexing in matrix representation, and not handling missing edges. Real code pattern uses for-loop over adjacency list.

examples.input: Graph adjacency list: {0:[1,2], 1:[2], 2:[]}. Iterate neighbors of vertex 0.
examples.trace: Access graph[0] = [1,2]. Iterate: first neighbor 1, process it. Next neighbor 2, process it. End.

examples.input: Graph adjacency list with weights: {0:[(1,5),(2,3)], 1:[(2,2)], 2:[]}. Iterate neighbors of vertex 0.
examples.trace: graph[0] = [(1,5),(2,3)]. Iterate: first (1,5), extract vertex 1 and weight 5. Next (2,3), extract vertex 2 and weight 3.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Graph Representation as Adjacency List
ladders.desc: Understand how to represent a graph using lists of neighbors for each vertex.
ladders.explanation: This foundation concept involves representing a graph in code, specifically using an adjacency list, which is essential for graph algorithms. The intuition is that a graph consists of vertices and edges; an adjacency list stores, for each vertex, a list of all vertices it is connected to (its neighbors). This representation is space-efficient for sparse graphs (O(V+E)) and allows quick access to neighbors. For example, in an undirected graph with vertices 0,1,2 and edges (0,1) and (1,2), the adjacency list is: 0: [1], 1: [0,2], 2: [1]. For directed graphs, edges are one-way: if edge from 0 to 1, then 0: [1], but 1 may not include 0. Weighted graphs extend this by storing pairs (neighbor, weight). Step-by-step to create: 1. Initialize a list of lists, size V. 2. For each edge (u,v), add v to list[u]; if undirected, also add u to list[v]. Time to build is O(E), space O(V+E). This feeds into L4 by providing the data structure for neighbor iteration. Common mistakes: not initializing lists for all vertices, forgetting to add both directions in undirected graphs, and incorrect indexing. Real code pattern uses list of lists or dictionary of lists.

examples.input: Graph with 3 vertices, edges: (0,1), (1,2). Represent as adjacency list.
examples.trace: Initialize list of 3 empty lists: [[], [], []]. Add edge (0,1) undirected: append 1 to list[0] and 0 to list[1]. Add edge (1,2) undirected: append 2 to list[1] and 1 to list[2]. Final: [[1], [0,2], [1]].

examples.input: Graph with 2 vertices, edge: (0,1) directed. Represent.
examples.trace: Initialize [[], []]. Add edge (0,1): append 1 to list[0] → [[1], []]. No reverse since directed. Final: [[1], []].

approaches.name: Dijkstra per Vertex O(V^3 log V)
approaches.summary: Run Dijkstra's algorithm from each vertex to compute shortest paths to all others, using a priority queue for efficiency. Suitable for graphs with non-negative edge weights.

ladders.title: Full Problem — All Pairs Shortest Paths via Dijkstra per Vertex
ladders.desc: Compute shortest paths between all vertex pairs in a weighted graph using Dijkstra's algorithm from each vertex.
ladders.explanation: This approach solves the all-pairs shortest path problem by running Dijkstra's algorithm from every vertex as the source. Dijkstra's algorithm efficiently finds shortest paths from a single source to all other vertices in graphs with non-negative edge weights using a priority queue. For each vertex v in the graph, we initialize distances from v to all others as infinity (except v to itself as 0), then use a min-heap to iteratively extract the vertex with smallest known distance, relax its outgoing edges, and update distances. After processing all vertices, we have a V×V distance matrix where dist[i][j] represents the shortest path from vertex i to j. The intuition is that by treating each vertex as an independent source, we can leverage Dijkstra's greedy optimal substructure property repeatedly. Step-by-step: 1) Initialize result matrix with INF. 2) For each vertex v: a) Set dist[v][v]=0, push (0,v) to heap. b) While heap not empty: pop (d,u); if d>dist[v][u], skip; for each neighbor w with weight wgt: if dist[v][u]+wgt<dist[v][w], update dist[v][w] and push (dist[v][w],w). 3) Return matrix. Time: O(V×(E log V)) but since E can be O(V²) in dense graphs, worst-case O(V³ log V). Space: O(V²) for matrix plus O(V) for heap. Connection to parent: This is the complete solution. Common mistakes: Not handling disconnected vertices (keep INF), using Dijkstra with negative weights (invalid), or inefficient heap operations. Real pattern: Use adjacency list with (neighbor, weight) pairs and heapq in Python.

examples.trace: Vertex 0 as source: dist[0]=[0,INF,INF]. Heap: (0,0). Pop (0,0): relax to 1 (4) and to 2 (1). Heap now has (1,2) and (4,1). Pop (1,2): no outgoing. Pop (4,1): 4+2=6 > current dist[0][2]=1, skip (lazy deletion). Final for source 0: [0,4,1].

Source 0: dist[0]=[0,INF,INF,INF]. Heap: (0,0). Pop (0,0): relax to 1: 0+5=5, update to 5, push (5,1); to 3: 0+10=10, update to 10, push (10,3). Heap: (5,1),(10,3). Pop (5,1): relax to 2: 5+3=8, update to 8, push (8,2). Heap: (8,2),(10,3). Pop (8,2): relax to 3: 8+1=9<10, update to 9, push (9,3). Pop (9,3): done. Final: [0,5,8,9]. Source 1: dist[1]=[INF,0,INF,INF]. Heap: (0,1). Pop (0,1): relax to 2: 0+3=3, update to 3, push (3,2). Pop (3,2): relax to 3: 3+1=4, update to 4, push (4,3). Pop (4,3): done. Final: [INF,0,3,4]. Source 2: dist[2]=[INF,INF,0,INF]. Heap: (0,2). Pop (0,2): relax to 3: 0+1=1, update to 1, push (1,3). Pop (1,3): done. Final: [INF,INF,0,1]. Source 3: dist[3]=[INF,INF,INF,0]. No edges, final: [INF,INF,INF,0]. Combine.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Single-Source Dijkstra with Priority Queue
ladders.desc: Find shortest paths from one source vertex to all others using a min-heap.
ladders.explanation: Dijkstra's algorithm computes shortest paths from a single source vertex s to all vertices in a graph with non-negative edge weights. The intuition is greedy: always expand the vertex with the smallest known distance first, as non-negative weights ensure this path cannot be improved later. It maintains a min-heap (priority queue) of (distance, vertex) pairs and a distance array dist where dist[v] is the current shortest distance from s to v. Initially, dist[s]=0, all others INF, and heap contains (0,s). While heap not empty: pop (d,u); if d>dist[u], skip (lazy deletion); for each neighbor v of u with edge weight w: if dist[u]+w<dist[v], update dist[v]=dist[u]+w and push (dist[v],v) to heap. This ensures we process vertices in increasing order of distance from s. Time: O((V+E) log V) with binary heap, as each vertex and edge may be processed once with heap operations O(log V). Space: O(V+E) for graph representation plus O(V) for heap and distances. Connection to level 0: This sub-routine is called V times to solve the all-pairs problem. Common mistakes: Not skipping popped vertices with outdated distances, using negative weights, or incorrect heap initialization. Real pattern: Use heapq.heappush/pop in Python with adjacency list.

examples.trace: Initialize dist=[0,INF,INF,INF], heap=[(0,0)]. Pop (0,0): relax edges: to 1: 0+2=2<INF, update dist[1]=2, push (2,1); to 2: 0+5=5<INF, update dist[2]=5, push (5,2). Heap: (2,1),(5,2). Pop (2,1): relax edges: to 2: 2+1=3<5, update dist[2]=3, push (3,2); to 3: 2+4=6<INF, update dist[3]=6, push (6,3). Heap: (3,2),(5,2),(6,3). Pop (3,2): relax edge to 3: 3+3=6 equals dist[3]=6, no update. Heap: (5,2),(6,3). Pop (5,2): d=5>dist[2]=3, skip. Pop (6,3): done. Final dist=[0,2,3,6].
Initialize dist=[INF,0,INF], heap=[(0,1)]. Pop (0,1): relax edge to 2: 0+2=2<INF, update dist[2]=2, push (2,2). Heap: (2,2). Pop (2,2): no outgoing edges. Final dist=[INF,0,2].

ladders.title: Core Logic — Relaxation of Edges in Shortest Path Algorithms
ladders.desc: Update shortest distance estimates by checking if a path through a vertex yields a shorter distance.
ladders.explanation: Relaxation is the fundamental operation in shortest path algorithms like Dijkstra. Given a current distance estimate dist[v] from source s to vertex v, and an edge from u to v with weight w, relaxation checks if traveling through u provides a shorter path to v: if dist[u] + w < dist[v], then update dist[v] = dist[u] + w. The intuition is that we maintain an upper bound on the shortest path distance, and relaxation tightens this bound as we discover better paths. In Dijkstra, after extracting vertex u with minimum dist[u] from the heap, we relax all its outgoing edges to potentially improve distances to its neighbors. This works because dist[u] is guaranteed to be the shortest distance to u at that point (due to non-negative weights and greedy selection), so any path through u cannot be improved later. Step-by-step: 1) Have arrays dist and adjacency list adj[u] = list of (v,w). 2) For each (v,w) in adj[u]: if dist[u] + w < dist[v], set dist[v] = dist[u] + w and update data structures (e.g., push to heap). Time per relaxation: O(1) for the check and update, but may trigger O(log V) heap operations. Space: O(1) additional. Connection to level 1: This is the inner loop of Dijkstra's algorithm. Common mistakes: Incorrect inequality (using <= instead of <, which is okay but less efficient), not handling INF correctly (treat INF as a large number), or relaxing edges from vertices whose distance is not finalized. Real pattern: Simple if-statement in a loop over neighbors.

examples.trace: Current dist: index 0=0, 1=5, 2=INF. For edge from u=1 to v=2 with weight w=3: check dist[1]+w=5+3=8. Compare to dist[2]=INF. Since 8<INF, update dist[2]=8. No other edges from u=1. Final dist=[0,5,8].

Current dist: [0,3,7]. Edge from u=0 to v=1 with w=2: dist[0]+w=0+2=2. Compare to dist[1]=3: 2<3, so update dist[1]=2. Edge to v=2 with w=6: 0+6=6. Compare to dist[2]=7: 6<7, update dist[2]=6. Final dist=[0,2,6].

ladders.title: Building Block — Min-Heap (Priority Queue) Operations for Dijkstra
ladders.desc: Use a min-heap to efficiently extract the vertex with smallest distance and update distances.
ladders.explanation: A min-heap (priority queue) is a data structure that allows O(log n) insertion and extraction of the minimum element. In Dijkstra's algorithm, it is used to always process the vertex with the smallest known distance next. The heap stores pairs (distance, vertex), ordered by distance. Operations: 1) Push: Insert a new pair with O(log n) time. 2) Pop: Remove and return the pair with smallest distance in O(log n) time. 3) Decrease-key: Update the distance of a vertex already in the heap; since many heap implementations don't support efficient decrease-key, we use lazy deletion by pushing new pairs and skipping outdated ones when popping. The intuition is that by maintaining a heap, we avoid scanning all vertices O(V) each time to find the minimum, reducing time from O(V²) to O((V+E) log V). Step-by-step in Dijkstra: Initialize heap with (0, source). While heap not empty: pop (d,u); if d > dist[u], skip; else process u and push updated distances for neighbors. Time per operation: O(log V) for push/pop. Space: O(V) for heap. Connection to level 2: The heap enables efficient selection of the next vertex to relax edges from. Common mistakes: Not using lazy deletion (causing heap size blow-up), incorrect ordering (distance first in tuple for min-heap), or not skipping popped vertices with larger distances. Real pattern: In Python, use heapq module with heappush and heappop.

examples.input: Initial heap: [(0,A)], operations: push (2,B), push (1,C), pop, push (3,D), pop, pop
examples.trace: Start heap: [(0,A)]. Push (2,B): heap becomes [(0,A),(2,B)]. Push (1,C): heap becomes [(0,A),(2,B),(1,C)]; heapify: (0,A) min, then (1,C) and (2,B). Pop: remove (0,A), heap now [(1,C),(2,B)]; return (0,A). Push (3,D): heap [(1,C),(2,B),(3,D)]. Pop: remove (1,C), heap [(2,B),(3,D)]; return (1,C). Pop: remove (2,B), heap [(3,D)]; return (2,B).

examples.input: Heap: [(5,X),(10,Y)], operations: push (3,Z), pop, push (7,X), pop, pop
examples.trace: Start heap: [(5,X),(10,Y)]. Push (3,Z): heap [(3,Z),(10,Y),(5,X)]; heapify: (3,Z) min. Pop: remove (3,Z), heap [(5,X),(10,Y)]; return (3,Z). Push (7,X): heap [(5,X),(10,Y),(7,X)]; note: (5,X) and (7,X) are separate entries for same vertex X. Pop: remove (5,X), heap [(7,X),(10,Y)]; return (5,X). Pop: remove (7,X), heap [(10,Y)]; return (7,X).

testCases.input: Heap: [(0,start)], push (2,a), push (1,b), pop, pop, pop
Heap: [(3,j),(5,k)], push (4,l), pop, push (2,m), pop, pop

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Graph Representation as Adjacency List
ladders.desc: Store a graph as a list of lists where each index has a list of neighboring vertices and edge weights.
ladders.explanation: An adjacency list is a way to represent a graph where each vertex has a list of its outgoing edges, typically as pairs (neighbor, weight). For a graph with V vertices, we create an array adj of size V, where adj[u] is a list of (v,w) for each edge from u to v with weight w. This representation is space-efficient for sparse graphs, using O(V+E) space. The intuition is that it directly captures connectivity without storing non-existent edges. Operations: 1) Add edge from u to v with weight w: append (v,w) to adj[u]. 2) Iterate neighbors of u: loop through adj[u]. 3) Check if edge exists: search adj[u] for v (O(degree(u)) time). Step-by-step creation: Initialize adj as list of empty lists for each vertex. For each edge (u,v,w), do adj[u].append((v,w)). Time to build: O(E). Space: O(V+E). Connection to level 3: Dijkstra uses adjacency list to efficiently relax edges from a vertex. Common mistakes: Off-by-one in vertex indexing (0-based vs 1-based), forgetting to store weights, or using adjacency matrix unnecessarily for sparse graphs. Real pattern: In Python, adj = [[] for _ in range(V)]; for u,v,w in edges: adj[u].append((v,w)).

examples.trace: Initialize adj as list of 3 empty lists: [[], [], []]. Process edge 0->1 weight=4: append (1,4) to adj[0], now adj[0]=[(1,4)]. Edge 0->2 weight=1: append (2,1) to adj[0], now adj[0]=[(1,4),(2,1)]. Edge 1->2 weight=2: append (2,2) to adj[1], now adj[1]=[(2,2)]. adj[2] remains empty. Final adj = [[(1,4),(2,1)], [(2,2)], []].

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Graph Vertices and Edges with Weights
ladders.desc: Understand a graph as a set of vertices connected by edges, each with a numerical weight.
ladders.explanation: A graph is a collection of vertices (nodes) and edges (connections between vertices). In weighted graphs, each edge has an associated numerical weight, often representing cost, distance, or time. Vertices are typically labeled with integers (0 to V-1). An edge is defined by a source vertex u, a target vertex v, and a weight w, written as u->v with weight w. The graph can be directed (edges have direction) or undirected (edges are bidirectional); here we focus on directed for shortest paths. The intuition is that graphs model networks like roads (vertices as cities, edges as roads with distances). Basic concepts: 1) Vertex: a point in the graph. 2) Edge: a connection from one vertex to another. 3) Weight: a number on an edge. 4) Path: a sequence of vertices connected by edges. 5) Shortest path: a path from vertex A to B with minimum total weight. Step-by-step: Given V=3, vertices are 0,1,2. An edge from 0 to 1 with weight 5 means traveling from vertex 0 to 1 costs 5. Time/space: Abstract concept, no complexity. Connection to level 4: This foundation is needed to create adjacency lists. Common mistakes: Confusing vertices with edges, misunderstanding weights (negative vs non-negative), or not handling direction. Real pattern: Think of vertices as indices in a list, edges as tuples (u,v,w).

examples.output: Graph with 3 vertices and 2 edges: from 0 to 1 (cost 4), from 1 to 2 (cost 3)
examples.trace: Vertices: 0, 1, 2. Edge 0->1: starts at vertex 0, ends at vertex 1, weight is 4. Edge 1->2: starts at vertex 1, ends at vertex 2, weight is 3. No edge from 2 to others. This represents a simple chain: 0 connected to 1 with cost 4, and 1 connected to 2 with cost 3.

examples.input: V=4, vertices: 0,1,2,3; edges: 0->2 weight=1, 2->3 weight=2, 3->1 weight=3
examples.trace: Vertices: 0, 1, 2, 3. Edge 0->2: from vertex 0 to vertex 2, weight 1. Edge 2->3: from vertex 2 to vertex 3, weight 2. Edge 3->1: from vertex 3 to vertex 1, weight 3. This forms a path 0->2->3->1 with total weight 1+2+3=6 from 0 to 1.

testCases.input: V=4, vertices: 0,1,2,3; edges: 0->1 weight=4, 0->2 weight=5, 1->3 weight=6
V=4, vertices: 0,1,2,3; edges: 0->1 weight=1, 1->2 weight=2, 2->3 weight=3, 3->0 weight=4

approaches.name: Floyd Warshall DP O(V^3)
approaches.summary: Use dynamic programming to iteratively update a distance matrix, considering all vertices as intermediate nodes. Handles graphs with negative weights (no negative cycles) and is straightforward to implement.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Floyd-Warshall All Pairs Shortest Path
ladders.desc: Complete implementation of Floyd-Warshall algorithm to find shortest paths between all vertex pairs in a weighted graph.
ladders.explanation: The Floyd-Warshall algorithm solves the all-pairs shortest path problem by employing dynamic programming. The core intuition is that the shortest path between any two vertices can be improved by considering all possible intermediate vertices. We maintain a distance matrix `dist` where `dist[i][j]` represents the shortest distance from vertex i to vertex j. Initially, this matrix contains direct edge weights (or INF for no edge, 0 for diagonal). The algorithm iterates through all vertices as potential intermediate points (k). For each pair (i, j), we check if going through vertex k provides a shorter path: `dist[i][j] = min(dist[i][j], dist[i][k] + dist[k][j])`. This works because when we consider vertex k as intermediate, we've already computed shortest paths using vertices 0 through k-1 as intermediates. The algorithm handles negative weights but fails with negative cycles (detectable by negative values on diagonal after algorithm). Time complexity is O(V³) from triple nested loops, space is O(V²) for the distance matrix. Connection to L1: This full solution uses the key DP update as its central operation. Common mistakes include incorrect initialization (missing INF values), off-by-one errors in vertex indexing, and not handling large INF values properly in comparisons. Real code pattern: Initialize dist matrix with direct edges, triple loop k,i,j updating distances, optionally check for negative cycles.

examples.trace: Initial dist: [[0,1,INF],[INF,0,2],[-1,INF,0]]. k=0: dist[2][1] = min(INF, -1+1) = 0. k=1: dist[0][2] = min(INF, 1+2) = 3. k=2: dist[1][0] = min(INF, 2 + (-1)) = 1. Final matrix: [[0,1,3],[1,0,2],[-1,0,0]]. Cycle 0→1→2→0 has total weight 1+2-1 = 2 > 0, so no negative cycle detected.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Dynamic Programming Update Step
ladders.desc: The core DP relaxation that checks if an intermediate vertex creates a shorter path between two vertices.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine implements the fundamental update operation of Floyd-Warshall: `dist[i][j] = min(dist[i][j], dist[i][k] + dist[k][j])`. The intuition is that if we've already computed shortest paths using vertices 0 through k-1 as intermediates, then considering vertex k as a new intermediate might provide a shorter path from i to j by going through k. This works because shortest path optimal substructure: if the shortest path from i to j goes through k, then the i-k and k-j subpaths must be shortest themselves. The algorithm for this sub-problem takes a current distance matrix, a vertex k, and updates all pairs (i,j) using k as intermediate. Time complexity for updating with one k is O(V²) as we iterate over all i,j pairs. Space is O(V²) for the matrix. Connection to L0: This update is repeated for all k from 0 to V-1 to build the complete solution. Common mistakes include updating in-place incorrectly (should use previous iteration's values for i,k and k,j), integer overflow with INF values, and not handling diagonal elements properly. Real code pattern: For given k, for i in range(V), for j in range(V): if dist[i][k] + dist[k][j] < dist[i][j]: update.

examples.trace: Initial matrix: [[0,3,INF],[INF,0,4],[1,INF,0]]. k=1. Only meaningful update: i=0 j=2 → dist[0][1] + dist[1][2] = 3 + 4 = 7 < INF → dist[0][2] = 7. No other pairs improve via vertex 1. Updated matrix: [[0,3,7],[INF,0,4],[1,INF,0]].

Start matrix: [[0,5,INF],[INF,0,1],[INF,INF,0]]. k=0. Check all i,j: i=0: dist[0][0]=0, so dist[0][0]+dist[0][j]=0+dist[0][j]. For j=0: 0+0=0 vs 0, no update. j=1: 0+5=5 vs 5, no update. j=2: 0+INF=INF vs INF, no update. i=1: dist[1][0]=INF, so all INF+something=INF, no updates. i=2: dist[2][0]=INF, so no updates. So matrix unchanged.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Intermediate Vertex Path Improvement
ladders.desc: The central insight that shortest paths can be improved by considering intermediate vertices.
ladders.explanation: This level focuses on the key logical insight behind Floyd-Warshall: the shortest path between two vertices i and j can be obtained by considering all possible intermediate vertices k. Formally, let d(i,j,k) be the shortest path from i to j using only vertices {0,1,...,k} as intermediates. Then d(i,j,k) = min(d(i,j,k-1), d(i,k,k-1) + d(k,j,k-1)). The intuition is that when we allow vertex k as an intermediate, we have two options: either the shortest path doesn't use k (so it's d(i,j,k-1)), or it does use k, in which case it consists of shortest paths from i to k and k to j using only vertices up to k-1 as intermediates. This optimal substructure allows dynamic programming. The algorithm for this sub-problem is to understand this recurrence and apply it conceptually. Time/space analysis isn't directly applicable here as it's a conceptual level. Connection to L1: This logic is implemented in the DP update step. Common mistakes include misunderstanding the order of intermediates (must process k from 0 to V-1), not seeing why the recurrence works, or confusing it with other shortest path algorithms. Real code pattern: This is the reasoning behind the triple nested loop structure.

examples.input: Vertices 0,1,2 with edges 0->1=3, 1->2=2, find shortest 0->2 using intermediate 1
examples.trace: Without intermediate 1: direct path 0->2 might not exist (INF). With intermediate 1 allowed: path 0->1->2 has length 3+2=5. So shortest path using {1} as intermediate is 5. According to recurrence: d(0,2,1) = min(d(0,2,0), d(0,1,0)+d(1,2,0)). d(0,2,0) is shortest using only vertex 0 as intermediate, which is just direct edge (INF). d(0,1,0)=3, d(1,2,0)=2. So min(INF, 3+2)=5.

examples.input: Vertices 0,1,2 with edges 0->1=1, 0->2=4, 1->2=2, find shortest 0->2 using intermediates {0,1}
examples.trace: We want d(0,2,1). d(0,2,0) is shortest using only vertex 0: direct edge 4. d(0,1,0)=1, d(1,2,0)=2. So d(0,2,1) = min(4, 1+2) = 3. So allowing vertex 1 as intermediate improves path from 4 to 3.

ladders.title: Building Block — Distance Matrix Representation
ladders.desc: Creating and initializing a 2D matrix to store shortest distances between all vertex pairs.
ladders.explanation: This sub-problem involves constructing the initial distance matrix for Floyd-Warshall. The matrix `dist` is a V×V 2D array where `dist[i][j]` represents the shortest known distance from vertex i to vertex j. Initialization: set `dist[i][i] = 0` for all i (distance to self is zero). For each directed edge from u to v with weight w, set `dist[u][v] = w`. All other entries are set to INF (a large number representing infinity/no path). This matrix will be updated iteratively. The intuition is that we start with direct edge distances and improve them by considering intermediates. Time complexity for initialization is O(V² + E) to fill matrix and set edges. Space is O(V²). Connection to L2: This matrix is the data structure on which the intermediate vertex logic operates. Common mistakes include using wrong INF value (too small causing overflow, too large causing issues in comparisons), forgetting to initialize diagonal to 0, incorrectly handling undirected edges (need to set both directions), or not using 0-based indexing consistently. Real code pattern: Create V×V matrix filled with INF, set diagonal to 0, for each edge (u,v,w): dist[u][v] = w.

examples.trace: Create 3x3 matrix, all INF. Set diagonal: dist[0][0]=0, dist[1][1]=0, dist[2][2]=0. Add edge 0->1 weight 4: dist[0][1]=4. Edge 1->2 weight 2: dist[1][2]=2. Edge 2->0 weight 1: dist[2][0]=1. All other entries remain INF.
Create 2x2 INF matrix. Set diagonal: dist[0][0]=0, dist[1][1]=0. Edge 0->1 weight 5: dist[0][1]=5. Edge 1->0 weight 3: dist[1][0]=3. No INF left.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Matrix Cell Update with Min Comparison
ladders.desc: The fundamental operation of comparing and updating a single cell in the distance matrix.
ladders.explanation: This is the simplest computational step in Floyd-Warshall: updating one cell `dist[i][j]` based on a potential new path through an intermediate vertex k. The operation is: `new_distance = dist[i][k] + dist[k][j]`. If `new_distance < dist[i][j]`, then update `dist[i][j] = new_distance`. Otherwise, leave it unchanged. The intuition is that we're checking if going from i to j via k is shorter than the current best known path. This is a direct application of the triangle inequality. Time complexity for one update is O(1). Space is O(1) aside from the matrix. Connection to L3: This operation is applied to every cell during matrix updates. Common mistakes include using `<=` instead of `<` (both work but `<` is standard), integer overflow when adding large numbers (especially with INF), and not handling INF correctly (INF + something should still be INF if INF represents a very large number). Real code pattern: if dist[i][k] != INF and dist[k][j] != INF and dist[i][k] + dist[k][j] < dist[i][j]: dist[i][j] = dist[i][k] + dist[k][j].

examples.trace: new_distance = dist[i][k] + dist[k][j] = 3 + 4 = 7. Since 7 < 8, update dist[i][j] = 7.
new_distance = INF + 2. Assuming INF is a very large number, INF+2 is still INF (or overflow). So new_distance is not less than 5. No update.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Graph Representation with Adjacency Matrix
ladders.desc: Understanding how to represent a weighted directed graph using a 2D matrix.
ladders.explanation: This foundational concept involves representing a graph with V vertices using a V×V adjacency matrix. Each cell `matrix[i][j]` stores the weight of the directed edge from vertex i to vertex j. If there is no edge from i to j, we store a special value INF (infinity). For the diagonal `matrix[i][i]`, we typically store 0 (distance from a vertex to itself is zero). This representation is dense and allows O(1) access to check if an edge exists and its weight. The intuition is that it's a natural way to encode all possible connections between vertices. Time complexity to create is O(V²) to initialize all entries. Space is O(V²). Connection to L4: This matrix structure is exactly what becomes the distance matrix in Floyd-Warshall. Common mistakes include confusing rows and columns (row index is source, column is destination), forgetting to handle large graphs where V² might be too big, or not understanding how to represent undirected graphs (set both matrix[i][j] and matrix[j][i] to weight). Real code pattern: Initialize V×V matrix with INF, set diagonal to 0, for each edge (u,v,w): matrix[u][v] = w.

examples.trace: Create 3x3 matrix. Fill with INF. Set matrix[0][0]=0, matrix[1][1]=0, matrix[2][2]=0. For edge 0->1 weight 2: set matrix[0][1]=2. For edge 1->2 weight 3: set matrix[1][2]=3. All other cells remain INF.

examples.input: V=2, undirected edge between 0 and 1 weight 5
examples.trace: Create 2x2 INF matrix. Set diagonal: matrix[0][0]=0, matrix[1][1]=0. Undirected edge means both directions: set matrix[0][1]=5 and matrix[1][0]=5.
